nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs with rubble or live coral cover; tends to stay near caves and crevices containing abundant invertebrate growth.

decorPreferences: Plenty of live rock with caves and crevices for foraging and refuge
Sand bed for occasional burying when resting
Tightly fitting lid or mesh cover — known jumper

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivorous diet of frozen mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine pellets, and quality flake. Continues to actively patrol live rock for small benthic crustaceans, pyramidellid snails, flatworms, and bristleworms, and has been used as a natural pest-control fish for these. Best fed at least twice daily.

goodWith: Hardy semi-aggressive to aggressive reef fish of similar or larger size
avoidWith: Other wrasses (especially smaller Pseudocheilinus and Cirrhilabrus species)
Royal Grammas and other timid basslets

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by cello caruso-turiello, 2048x1366. Tack-sharp in-situ reef shot; whole fish framed, vivid orange-red body with the eight dark horizontal lines and fine orange facial spotting clearly shown. Best specimen.
Photo by Debra Baker, 2048x1737. Sharp diagonal in-reef view; eight lines and orange facial dots visible, yellowish tail. Good alternate angle.
Photo by jeyre, 2048x1536. Sharp full-body profile; correct ID and reef context, though coloration is paler and the body stripes are more muted than the recommended pick.

summary: The Eightline Wrasse is a vividly striped reef fish with eight horizontal body lines, widely reported as aggressive toward smaller tankmates and other wrasses, and valued as a natural control for pest worms and pyramidellid snails.
careNotes: The Eightline Wrasse (Pseudocheilinus octotaenia) is a hardy pygmy wrasse in the same genus as the Six Line Wrasse but is generally regarded as more aggressive and grows noticeably larger, reaching roughly 12–14 cm as an adult. This size and temperament combination tends to intensify with maturity; the species is widely reported to be aggressive toward other wrasses, smaller peaceful fishes such as firefish, dartfish, small gobies, and mandarin dragonets, and to prey opportunistically on ornamental shrimp, small crabs, and snails. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per tank, to introduce it last when stocking the display, and to provide extensive rockwork so that weaker tankmates can break line-of-sight and find refuge. Although broadly considered reef-safe with corals and large sessile invertebrates, it is best treated as reef-safe with caution because of its appetite for motile invertebrates. The species is native to a wide Indo-West Pacific range stretching from East Africa, including the Comoros and Seychelles, east to Hawaii and Ducie Island, and is found on seaward reefs from about 2–50 m depth, typically around caves and crevices with rich invertebrate growth. Like other wrasses, it is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male over time. It is also a known jumper, so a tightly-fitting lid or mesh cover is generally recommended.
breedingNotes: In the wild, the Eightline Wrasse spawns as an oviparous species with distinct male-female pairing, releasing pelagic eggs that drift in open water. Captive breeding has not been commonly documented.
